subtitle: Visit counter with bot & AI-crawler awareness.
top_ai_empty: No AI crawlers seen in this range.
top_uas_subtitle: What's actually hitting your site, with the classifier's verdict for each. Use it to spot bots that pretend to be browsers.
history_subtitle: Aggregated totals from purged months. Page paths and referrers are not preserved at this granularity.
history_top_ai_empty: No AI crawlers in the archive yet.
guide_intro_body: A minimalist single-site visit counter written in plain PHP 8.1+ and SQLite. It classifies every hit as a human, an official search-engine bot, an AI crawler or another generic bot, and shows the result on a single dashboard. No Composer, no npm, no build step — just drop it on any PHP host.
guide_install_step1: Copy config.example.php to config.php and edit the values. Set salt to a long random string and site_host to your domain (e.g. example.com).
guide_install_step2: Include shield.php at the very top of any PHP page you want to track: require __DIR__ . '/path/to/shield.php';. Errors are swallowed silently — the tracker can never break the host page.
guide_install_step3: For non-PHP sites (static HTML, other languages, cross-domain tracking), call api.php instead — as a 1×1 image (<img src="api.php?p=/about&h=example.com">) or via fetch(). See the comments at the top of api.php for full examples.
guide_install_step4: Open index.php in your browser. The SQLite database file and its tables are created automatically on the first hit.
guide_embed_intro: For static HTML sites or non-PHP backends, paste this 1×1 tracking pixel anywhere inside your page's <body>. No JavaScript or cookies required — every page load is recorded server-side.
guide_embed_path_note: The page path is auto-detected from the Referer header. To set it explicitly, append &p=/your-path to the URL.
guide_embed_no_site_host: Set site_host in config.php first — your ready-to-paste snippet will appear here.
guide_embed_cli: Open this page over HTTP to see the ready-to-paste snippet with your real install URL.
guide_dashboard_intro: Everything is computed on demand from the registros table. Each row is a single page hit with the visitor's classification, the IP hash, the path, the referrer and the language.
guide_dashboard_cards_body: Hits today, hits in the last 7 and 30 days, and unique visitors over the last 7 days. Unique counts distinct ip_hash values — the same IP across two pageviews counts as one unique visitor.
guide_dashboard_charts_body: Hits per hour: stacked bars over the last 24 hours, separated by visitor type. Visitor mix: donut with the proportion of humans, official bots, AI bots and other bots within the selected range.
guide_dashboard_tables_body: Top AI crawlers (only rows where kind = bot_ai), top pages visited by humans (so bot traffic doesn't pollute the ranking), and top referrers grouped by host (so https://x.com/a and https://x.com/b merge into a single x.com row).
guide_caveat_body: Classification by User-Agent is a heuristic, not a verification. The User-Agent header is set by the client and can be spoofed trivially. A request that claims to be GPTBot or ClaudeBot may actually be a script pretending to be one — and a real browser can be configured to send a misleading User-Agent. For high-confidence bot identification you would need reverse-DNS lookups or IP allowlists from each crawler's official documentation, both outside the scope of this minimalist tool. Treat the dashboard's bot labels as an informed estimate, not a fact.
guide_privacy_body: Visitor IPs are stored in two columns: ip (raw) and ip_hash (sha256 of salt + ip). Set store_ip = false in config.php to drop the raw IP entirely — unique-visitor counts keep working because they rely only on the hash. The auto-purge aggregates completed months into resumenes_mensuales and removes those rows from registros once it grows past purge_max_rows. The current calendar month is never purged.
guide_security_intro: By default the dashboard is public — anyone who knows the URL can read your stats. Two ways to lock it down, from quick to robust:
guide_security_inapp_body: Fill auth_user and auth_password_hash in config.php. The dashboard and this guide will then prompt for credentials via the browser's native auth dialog. Generate the password hash from the command line:
guide_security_https_warning: HTTPS is mandatory. HTTP Basic Auth sends the credentials in base64 on every single request. Over plain HTTP they are trivially recoverable on the wire.
guide_security_logout_note: There is no logout button. HTTP Basic Auth has no real logout — close the browser tab (or all tabs for this host) to clear the cached credentials.
guide_security_server_body: More robust because the gate runs before any PHP code. Below is an Apache .htaccess snippet that locks the entire mini-guardianweb directory. Drop the file at the install root and adjust the path:
guide_security_htpasswd_hint: Create the .htpasswd file outside the web root with: htpasswd -c /path/to/.htpasswd yourusername
guide_about_body: This mini version is offered by guardianweb.es. If you want a full panel — with verified bot detection, multi-site support, longer history and richer analytics — you can register for free.
